skill.name: architecture-decision-records
skill.description: Create and maintain Architecture Decision Records that document technical choices, context, trade-offs, and consequences.
skill.summary: Create clear Architecture Decision Records for important technical decisions.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, and weak cryptography patterns, but review found documentation-only Markdown examples and reference links. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, automatic execution path, or cryptographic implementation was found in the single SKILL.md file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged fenced Markdown blocks, inline code, and one adr-tools example block as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines are documentation examples, and the skill has no executable file or automatic command runner. The adr-tools commands are manual instructions that users should review before running.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows Markdown code fences, inline code, or a bash documentation block. The reviewed skill contains no implementation that invokes these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Flagged as Network Access
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are documentation references for PostgreSQL, Event Sourcing, ADR templates, and adr-tools. No code performs network requests or sends local data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each flagged URL appears in a References or Resources section. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or network-capable script in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are Prose Collisions
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography detections are false positives from ADR prose and headings. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, encryption API, or credential-handling logic is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain natural-language ADR content such as decision, design, accepted, and superseded. No evidence of cryptographic implementation was found.

content.value_statement: Important technical choices are often lost in chat threads and tickets. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create concise ADRs that preserve context, options, decisions, and consequences.
actual_capabilities: Explains when an Architecture Decision Record is appropriate.
Provides standard, lightweight, Y-statement, deprecation, and RFC-style ADR templates.
Shows how to structure ADR directories, indexes, statuses, and related decisions.
Gives review checklists for context, options, consequences, security, cost, and reversibility.
Includes optional adr-tools command examples for teams that use that utility.
limitations: It does not validate architecture decisions against a live codebase automatically.
It does not create files unless the assistant using it performs separate file edits.
It does not replace review by senior engineers or affected teams.
The adr-tools commands are examples and require local tooling to be installed.

use_cases.title: Record a New Platform Decision
use_cases.description: Create an ADR for a database, framework, API, or infrastructure choice before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Standardize Engineering Documentation
use_cases.description: Define an ADR template, status model, and review checklist for consistent technical decision records.

use_cases.title: Review Historical Trade-offs
use_cases.description: Document why a past decision should be deprecated, superseded, or kept after new constraints emerge.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic ADR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a single clear decision.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a lightweight ADR for choosing [technology] for [project]. Include status, date, context, decision, and consequences.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a standard ADR for [decision]. Compare [option A], [option B], and [option C]. Include decision drivers, pros, cons, rationale, and risks.

prompt_templates.title: Supersede an Existing ADR
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for replacing an older decision.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ADR that supersedes ADR-[number]. Explain what changed, the new decision, migration steps, consequences, and lessons learned.

prompt_templates.title: Design an ADR Process
prompt_templates.prompt: Define an ADR workflow for [team or organization]. Include directory structure, status definitions, review checklist, ownership, and maintenance rules.

output_examples.input: We need to choose a primary database for an e-commerce service.
output: A standard ADR with status, context, decision drivers, considered options, rationale, consequences, risks, and related decisions.
The record explains why PostgreSQL fits transaction integrity, search, JSON attributes, and team experience.

output_examples.input: Our team wants a lightweight way to document frontend decisions.
output: A concise ADR with status, date, deciders, context, decision, good outcomes, bad outcomes, and mitigations.
The record is short enough for a pull request while preserving the reason for the decision.

output_examples.input: We need to replace an old storage decision with a new one.
output: A superseding ADR that cites the older record, explains changed constraints, states the new decision, and outlines migration phases.
The record keeps historical context while making the current direction clear.

best_practices: Write ADRs before implementation so reviewers can challenge assumptions early.
Keep each ADR focused on one significant decision and its concrete consequences.
Use superseding ADRs instead of rewriting accepted records after facts change.
anti_patterns: Do not use ADRs for routine bug fixes, small configuration changes, or minor version upgrades.
Do not hide rejected options or negative consequences from future readers.
Do not leave accepted ADRs disconnected from implementation tickets or related documentation.

faq.question: What is an Architecture Decision Record?
faq.answer: An ADR is a short document that captures the context, decision, rationale, and consequences of an important technical choice.

faq.question: When should I write an ADR?
faq.answer: Write one for significant architecture, technology, integration, security, or API decisions that future teams must understand.

faq.question: Can this skill help with deprecated decisions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a deprecation template that explains changed constraints, migration steps, consequences, and lessons learned.

faq.question: Does this skill enforce a single ADR format?
faq.answer: No. It includes standard, lightweight, Y-statement, deprecation, and RFC-style formats for different levels of detail.

faq.question: Does this skill run adr-tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It only documents optional adr-tools commands that a user may choose to run manually.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides documentation patterns that Claude Code, Codex, and Claude can use when drafting or maintaining ADRs.
